body:
**Operating System**
Linux -  Ubuntu 25.10
Kernel: 6.18.2-x64v3-xanmod1
KDE Plasma 6.4.5

**Version**
2.1.0 - compiled locally

**Describe the bug**
This system has four different bluetooth adapters.   It seems like OpenSCQ30  can only detect devices paired on one of the adapters even though normal operation (like playing audio) works regardless of which adapter is paired with the anker speaker.


**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Run the blueman bluetooth manager from the application launcher
2. Press "Search" button 
3. Wait for anker device to appear in the list and then connect to it

4. Run OpenSCQ30-gui and try to "add device" - if you are lucky it will show up in the list and you can proceed as normal,  if you are unlucky the list will be empty
5. In bluetooth manager remove device
6. In bluetooth manager use "Adapter" menu to choose a different bluetooth adapter and repeat starting at step 2 above

**Additional context**
I don't think it matters, but I only have an x600 which is not currently supported.  But I was able to get a status packet from it by walking through each adapter on the system and pairing it one by one until OpenSCQ30 was able to detect it.  I've submitted a separate PR requesting support for the x600 with that status packet.
title: Support for Multiple Bluetooth Adapters on Linux

title: ARM build for linux

body: Looks like a lot changed since the last release – the universal APK tripled in size. So we tried to build the arm64 one (with the goal to switch to that). But RB failed with a minimal difference in `libopenscq30_android.so`: (full diffs: [diff.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24266446/diff.zip)). Recipe used: ```yaml - tag: v2.0.0 apks: - apk_pattern: openscq30-android-arm64-v8a\.apk apk_url: https://github.com/Oppzippy/OpenSCQ30/releases/download/v2.0.0/openscq30-android-arm64-v8a.apk build: - RUSTUP_VER="$(grep rustup .build-tool-versions | awk '{print $2}')" - RUST_TOOLCHAIN="$(grep -E 'rust\b' .build-tool-versions | awk '{print $2}')" - CARGO_VER="$(grep cargo-ndk .build-tool-versions | awk '{print $2}')" - git clone https://github.com/rust-lang/rustup.git -b $RUSTUP_VER - export ANDROID_NDK=/opt/sdk/ndk/${PROVISIONING_NDK} - ${BUILD_REPO_DIR}/rustup/rustup-init.sh -y --default-toolchain=$RUST_TOOLCHAIN --target armv7-linux-androideabi --target aarch64-linux-android --target i686-linux-android --target x86_64-linux-android - source $HOME/.cargo/env - cargo install cargo-ndk --version $CARGO_VER - cd android - ./gradlew assembleRelease - find . -name '*.apk' - mv app/build/outputs/apk/release/app-arm64-v8a-release*.apk /outputs/unsigned.apk build_cpus: build_home_dir: /home/runner build_repo_dir: /home/runner/work/OpenSCQ30/OpenSCQ30 build_timeout: build_user: build provisioning: android_home: /opt/sdk build_tools: cmake: cmdline_tools: version: '19.0' url: https://dl.google.com/android/repository/commandlinetools-linux-13114758_latest.zip sha256: 7ec965280a073311c339e571cd5de778b9975026cfcbe79f2b1cdcb1e15317ee extra_packages: - make - g++ - cmake - protobuf-compiler image: debian:trixie-slim jdk: openjdk-21-jdk-headless ndk: 29.0.14206865 platform: platform_tools: tools: verify_gradle_wrapper: true ``` (I've tried the universal one as well, same issue – though strangely, while the sha256sum of your arm64 lib is identical, the one of our build is not (despite of the diff looking pretty much the same: a D turning into an E); did we hit some non-determinism here? We usually try building in a loop then, in the hope to draw a lucky number – but with a single round taking almost 20 minutes, that's not really a good option here. With a single-ABI build, it might be worth a try). [diff.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24266915/diff.zip) Questions: 1. Do you have any idea what we might have missed there, or what went wrong? We've built from the tagged commit; but as the APK does not contain valid `vcsInfo`, we cannot verify which commit yours was built from 2. Is there a way to skip building "unwanted" ABIs? I've thought about something like passing `-x :app:cargo-build-release-armeabi-v7a -x app:rust-deploy-release-armeabi-v7a` etc. to the `gradlew` call – but that's not the proper way (there might be dependencies, and your steps might change in the future as well). 3. Is it OK for you if we switch to the arm64 variant? It would allow us to keep multiple versions (here: 3), while with the universal one, our size limit would only permit a single one. Thanks in advance for your help!
title: Reproducible Build fails for v2.0.0
